title: “And what, monks, is the noble search? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to birth, having known the peril in what is subject to…
content: “And what, monks, is the noble search? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to birth, having known the peril in what is subject to birth, seeks the unborn, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna; being himself subject to aging, having known the peril in aging, seeks the unaging, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna; being subject to sickness … the unailing … subject to death … the deathless … subject to sorrow … the sorrowless … subject to defilement, having known the peril in defilement, seeks the undefiled, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna. This, monks, is called the noble search.”

title: “And what, monks, is the practice of calming? Here, monks, a monk does not consent to arisen sensual thought; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and…
content: “And what, monks, is the practice of calming? Here, monks, a monk does not consent to arisen sensual thought; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and makes it non-existent. He does not consent to arisen ill-will thought … harmful thought … to arisen evil, unwholesome thoughts; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and makes them non-existent. This, monks, is called the practice of calming.”

title: - “Friend, I do not abandon the present to chase after things subject to time. But, friend, I abandon things subject to time to chase…
content: - “Friend, I do not abandon the present to chase after things subject to time. But, friend, I abandon things subject to time to chase after the present. The Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are subject to time, of great suffering, of great despair — the peril in them is even greater. This Dhamma is visible here and now, not subject to time, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be directly experienced by the wise.”

title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who has completely cut off all searches? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned the search for sensual pleasures,…
content: “And how, monks, is a monk one who has completely cut off all searches? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned the search for sensual pleasures, abandoned the search for existence, and the search for the holy life has been calmed. Thus, monks, is a monk who has completely cut off all searches. And how, monks, is a monk one whose thoughts are unblemished? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned sensual thought, abandoned ill-will thought, abandoned harmful thought. Thus, monks, is a monk whose thoughts are unblemished. And how, monks, is a monk one whose mental activities are calmed? Here, monks, a monk, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain, with the previous passing away of joy and grief, enters and dwells in the fourth jhāna, neither painful nor pleasant, with equanimity and mindfulness purified. Thus, monks, is a monk whose mental activities are calmed. And how, monks, is a monk one whose mind is well liberated? Here, monks, a monk’s mind is liberated from greed, liberated from hatred, liberated from delusion. Thus, monks, is a monk whose mind is well liberated. And how, monks, is a monk one whose wisdom is well liberated? Here, monks, a monk knows: ‘Greed has been abandoned in me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, unable to arise in the future’; knows: ‘Hatred has been abandoned in me …’; knows: ‘Delusion has been abandoned in me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, unable to arise in the future.’ Thus, monks, is a monk whose wisdom is well liberated. Monks, all those noble ones in the past who lived the noble dwelling — they lived these ten noble dwellings. All those noble ones in the future who will live the noble dwelling — they will live these ten noble dwellings. All those noble ones in the present who live the noble dwelling — they live these ten noble dwellings. These, monks, are the ten noble dwellings that the noble ones have lived, are living, and will live.”

title: “Monks, I too, before my enlightenment, while still a bodhisatta, being myself subject to birth sought what is subject to birth; being subject to aging…
content: “Monks, I too, before my enlightenment, while still a bodhisatta, being myself subject to birth sought what is subject to birth; being subject to aging sought what is subject to aging; being subject to sickness … subject to death … subject to sorrow … subject to defilement sought what is subject to defilement. Then, monks, I thought: ‘Why do I, being subject to birth, seek what is subject to birth? … Suppose that I, being subject to birth, having known the peril in what is subject to birth, seek the unborn, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna; being subject to aging … the unaging … subject to sickness … the unailing … subject to death … the deathless … subject to sorrow … the sorrowless … subject to defilement, having known the peril in defilement, seek the undefiled, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna.’”

title: “Monks, for a clansman who has gone forth out of faith, this is the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma: dwell often in disenchantment toward…
content: “Monks, for a clansman who has gone forth out of faith, this is the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma: dwell often in disenchantment toward form … toward feeling … toward perception … toward formations, dwell often in disenchantment toward consciousness. One who dwells often in disenchantment toward form … toward consciousness fully understands form … feeling … perception … formations … fully understands consciousness. One who fully understands form, fully understands feeling, fully understands perception, fully understands formations, fully understands consciousness, is freed from form, freed from feeling, freed from perception, freed from formations, freed from consciousness, freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is freed from suffering.”

title: “Nāgita, for those who are heavily inclined toward delight, when there is change, alteration, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise — that is the…
content: “Nāgita, for those who are heavily inclined toward delight, when there is change, alteration, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise — that is the natural result. For those who fix their minds on the sign of the foul, Nāgita, disenchantment with the sign of the beautiful becomes established — that is the natural result. For one who dwells contemplating impermanence in the six sense bases, Nāgita, disenchantment with contact becomes established — that is the natural result. For one who dwells contemplating arising and passing away in the five aggregates of clinging, Nāgita, disenchantment with clinging becomes established — that is the natural result.”

title: “Therefore, Māgandiya, associate with true persons. By associating with true persons, you will hear the true Dhamma. By hearing the true Dhamma, you will live…
content: “Therefore, Māgandiya, associate with true persons. By associating with true persons, you will hear the true Dhamma. By hearing the true Dhamma, you will live in accordance with the Dhamma and the proper Dhamma. By living in accordance with the Dhamma and the proper Dhamma, you will know and see for yourself: ‘These are diseases, boils, darts. Here, these diseases, boils, darts are abandoned without remainder. Because clinging ceases in me, becoming ceases; because becoming ceases, birth ceases; because birth ceases, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair cease. Thus is the cessation of this whole mass of suffering.’”

title: “Here, monks, a person truly understands the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to sensual pleasures. By truly understanding…
content: “Here, monks, a person truly understands the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in regard to sensual pleasures. By truly understanding them, sensual desire, sensual delight, sensual attachment, sensual infatuation, sensual thirst, sensual fever, sensual clinging, sensual craving regarding sensual pleasures do not invade and remain. This, monks, is called freedom from the yoke of sensual desire.”

title: “Sunakkhatta, I give this simile to make the meaning clear. The meaning here is this: The wound, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the six sense-bases…
content: “Sunakkhatta, I give this simile to make the meaning clear. The meaning here is this: The wound, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the six sense-bases (phassayatana). The poison, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for ignorance. The dart, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for craving. The probing tool, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for mindfulness. The knife, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for noble wisdom. The surgeon, Sunakkhatta, is a synonym for the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. Indeed, Sunakkhatta, a monk who guards himself in regard to the six sense-bases, knowing that ‘Attachment (upadhi) is the root of suffering,’ becomes unattached and liberated by the destruction of attachment. It does not happen that he would still focus his body on attachment or direct his mind to attachment.”
